title: Space Tech Briefing — July 07, 2026
summary:
Good morning, space enthusiasts, and welcome to your daily dose of cosmic news! It's Tuesday, July 07, 2026, and we've got a packed briefing for you, from asteroid encounters to out-of-this-world summer style! Let's dive in!

First up, a little something for those who love to wear their passion for space. Want to inject some cosmic style into your summer? The French space agency CNES has just unveiled its 'summer essentials' collection! Get ready to rock those vacation vibes with their stylish gear, all in the vibrant colors of the agency. And here's the best part: there's a special summer offer running from July 1st all the way through August 15th. Perfect for showing off your love for space exploration!

And now, from the farthest reaches of our solar system! The incredible Japanese Hayabusa-2 probe made another historic pass! On Sunday, July 5th, it successfully flew over the asteroid known as Torifune, officially designated (98943) 2001 CC21. JAXA, the Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency, released the first stunning images yesterday, July 6th. This is just another triumph for a mission launched back in late 2014. Hayabusa-2 has already made history, rendezvoused with asteroid Ryugu in 2018, collected precious samples in 2019, and brought them safely back to Earth in late 2020. Thanks to remarkable fuel savings, the mission has been extended as 'Hayabusa2#'. What an incredible journey!

But wait, there's more asteroid action! China's Tianwen-2 probe has also reached its initial cosmic destination! On July 5th, 2026, Tianwen-2 successfully arrived at the asteroid 2016HO3 Kamo’oalewa. The Chinese National Space Administration, CNSA, has already shared the first incredible images taken during the probe's close approach, just 20 kilometers from the asteroid's surface! This mission launched on May 29th, 2025, embarking on a remarkable 400-day journey, covering approximately a million kilometers. Along the way, Tianwen-2 performed crucial trajectory corrections. It first detected the asteroid on June 6th, entered a co-planar trajectory at 30,000 kilometers on June 7th, and by June 19th, it was within a mere 2,000 kilometers. Precision at its finest!

What a day for space exploration! Two incredible asteroid encounters and a chance to get your hands on some stellar summer gear. That's all for your morning space briefing on this July 7th. Thank you for tuning in, and keep looking up!
